content.full_text:
84300

July 16, 1976

Anthony L. Hodges, Attorney
Continental Oil Company
P.O. Box 2197
Houston, Texas 77001

Dear Mr. Hodges:

In response to your letter of July 1, 1976, I'm sending to you enclosed a copy of the Political Reform Act of 1974 which includes 1975 and 1976 legislative amendments. The campaign disclosure provisions begin at Section 84200.

The following are answers to your specific questions in the order presented:

1. California law does not prohibit corporate contributions to state candidates nor does it limit the amount of contributions.

2. California law does not prohibit a corporation from establishing or maintaining a political committee for the support of state candidates.

3. While California law does not prohibit a corporation from establishing or maintaining a political committee for the support of federal candidates, federal law prohibits corporate contributions to candidates for federal office. You should contact the Federal Elections Commission at 1325 19th Street, N.W., Washington, D.C. 20463, 800/424-9530, for information in this regard.

4. California law does not prohibit a political committee from using a payroll deduction plan to collect voluntary contributions from the corporate employees of its sponsors.

5. California law does not prohibit a corporation or its political committee from contributing to or supporting issues or ballot questions.

For further information regarding campaign disclosure reporting requirements, I'm enclosing an information manual and forms used by political committees.

If you have further questions, please contact our staff.

Sincerely,

Nan Hambleton
Special Compliance Representative

Anthony L. Hodges                                    Continental Oil Company
Attorney                                                      P. O. Box 2197
                                                             Houston, Texas 77001


July 1, 1976




Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Sir:

     I am writing on behalf of the Conoco Employees' Good Government
Fund which is a multi-candidate political committee organized
in the State of Texas for the support of candidates for federal
office.

     The purpose of this letter is to obtain guidance so that the
committee and its sponsor corporation will not violate the
election laws of your state.  I would appreciate a response
to the following questions at your earliest convenience:

     (1)  Does any California law prohibit a corporation
          from making a political contribution to a state
          candidate? If so, are there limits upon the
          amount which can be contributed?

     (2)  Does any California law prohibit a corporation from
          establishing or maintaining a political committee
          for the support of state candidates?

     (3)  Does any California law prohibit a corporation from
          establishing or maintaining a political committee
          for the support of federal candidates?

     (4)  Does any California law prohibit a political committee
          from using a payroll deduction plan to collect
          voluntary contributions from the corporate em-
          ployees of its sponsor?

     (5)  Does any California law prohibit a corporation or
          its political committee from contributing to or
          supporting issues or ballot questions?

The image appears to be an almost entirely blank white page with two small circular marks or holes visible near the top (approximately where binding holes might be), and some very faint marks or artifacts in the upper right corner. The rest of the page is blank with no legible text to transcribe.

If the subject matter covered by the above questions is beyond
the scope of your jurisdiction, please forward this request to
the appropriate agency or to the office of the Attorney
General. Also I would appreciate receiving any new provisions
in the election laws which may have been passed in 1976.

Thank you for your attention and cooperation in this matter.

Very truly yours,

Anthony L. Hodges

Anthony L. Hodges
